caveats: a: No six-digit occupation table or wage-weighted task study was available.
a: Current automated process control and laboratory instruments are excluded from not-yet-automated opportunity.
rho: No representative LMM synthetic-colorant AI adoption study was found.
rho: The range excludes autonomous release decisions and hazardous process control.
e: Eligibility is inferred from the industry business model, not observed for the injected firms.
e: The merged code spans organic and inorganic products with different capital intensity and end markets.
s5: The source is cross-industry and includes firms below the LMM lens.
s5: Stated owner plans are not completed qualifying control transfers.
q: No representative LMM contract sample was available.
q: Retention differs sharply between commodity white pigments and bespoke organic or performance colorants.
d5: TiO2 is only one pigment family and Kronos is global.
d5: ACC forecasts are one-year and broader than synthetic dyes and pigments.
d5: The share of industry demand exposed to food-dye policy is not observed.
o: Operator persistence is inferred from physical and compliance requirements rather than measured directly.
o: Food applications may shift entirely to natural colors outside the current operator basket.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B product sales under customer specifications, purchase orders, distributor relationships, qualification cycles, and some supply arrangements; price per unit is supported by color performance, consistency, technical service, and reliable availability.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal colorant plants, natural-color producers outside the code, electrostatic and photographic toners outside the definition, pure distributors, integrated operations outside the LMM band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly supply synthetic organic or inorganic dyes, pigments, lakes, and toners to external coatings, plastics, inks, textiles, paper, and other industrial or specialty customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for all chemical manufacturing, not 325130, and headcount rather than task exposure. I map portions of color matching and formulation analysis, laboratory and quality documentation, technical-service research, regulatory records, planning, procurement, quoting, sales, and office work to AI-addressable tasks, leaving reactors, milling, filtration, drying, packaging, sampling, maintenance, and hands-on testing mostly unexposed. Smaller specialty dye and pigment firms likely have more technical-service and batch-documentation labor than the broader chemical population, so the proxy may bias exposure downward.
caveats: No six-digit occupation table or wage-weighted task study was available.
Current automated process control and laboratory instruments are excluded from not-yet-automated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted role and task census from 10-15 U.S. LMM dye and pigment manufacturers, including current digital automation, color-matching, laboratory, quality, regulatory, commercial, and production work, would materially change exposure.
a.rationale: Pigment producers employ scientists, chemists, engineers, and technicians for process technology, quality assurance, product development, and application support alongside production labor. AI can accelerate information-heavy and analytic tasks but cannot perform the physical batch processes or most laboratory handling.

d5.bridge: Kronos cites long-run global TiO2 consumption growth of 2-3% but reported weak 2025 demand and cyclicality; ACC forecasts only 0.4% U.S. coatings output growth and a 0.2% specialty-chemical decline in 2026. These sources cover segments and a global commodity pigment, not five-year constant-quality U.S. demand for all 325130 firms. I use about 0.4% annual base growth, below the global history, with a wide cyclical range. Global TiO2 history likely biases U.S. growth upward.
caveats: TiO2 is only one pigment family and Kronos is global.
ACC forecasts are one-year and broader than synthetic dyes and pigments.
The share of industry demand exposed to food-dye policy is not observed.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year U.S. shipment-volume forecasts by inorganic pigment, organic pigment, textile dye, ink, coatings, plastics, paper, and food colorants, adjusted for formulation intensity and customer inventory, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Colorants remain required in coatings, plastics, paper, inks, textiles, and many specialty products, while housing, industrial production, trade, customer inventories, substitution, and Chinese capacity drive cycles. FDA's phase-out of petroleum-based synthetic food dyes is a specific demand loss for food-color producers, though food is only one application subset.

caveats: Eligibility is inferred from the industry business model, not observed for the injected firms.
The merged code spans organic and inorganic products with different capital intensity and end markets.
e.change_evidence: A coded audit of the 23 injected firms using ownership, manufacturing assets, product chemistry, customer mix, recurring revenue, and captive status would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: Repeat external supply and technical support are core to dye and pigment manufacturing, so most independent LMM firms qualify. Exclusions arise from captive production, misclassified distributors, natural-color firms outside the code, and operations that lack independent transferable customer relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325130 ($949,029,137) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325130 ($6,752,973,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $855,240 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325130); class <5: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,191,538 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $214,596 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,392,643 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $430,915 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,082,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $915,268 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,384,696 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,230,484 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $123,335,071 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,212,646 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $330,075,357 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,446,572 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 23

caveats: Operator persistence is inferred from physical and compliance requirements rather than measured directly.
Food applications may shift entirely to natural colors outside the current operator basket.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and formulation data quantifying natural-color substitution, in-house colorant production, pigment extenders, uncolored materials, digital-print chemistry changes, and direct imports would materially change persistence.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five synthetic colorant quantity requires an accountable manufacturer to synthesize or precipitate, mill, separate, dry, test, document, and release a physical chemical product. Software may optimize formulation and matching but cannot replace the pigment or regulated process; some demand can shift to natural colors, alternative materials, or customer in-sourcing.

q.bridge: Kronos is a large global titanium-dioxide producer, not the LMM dye and pigment population. It reports negotiated pricing, competition on price, quality, service, stability and high-performance grades, and a shift toward customer-specific differentiated pigments. I infer greater retention for qualified specialty colorants and lower retention for commodity grades; Kronos's commodity-heavy mix likely biases the base downward for specialty LMM firms, while its scale may bias bargaining power upward.
caveats: No representative LMM contract sample was available.
Retention differs sharply between commodity white pigments and bespoke organic or performance colorants.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and SKU-level contract, invoice, rebate, renewal, churn, qualification, and gross-margin data before and after measurable productivity changes would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Customer-specific performance, qualification, batch consistency, application support, and switching risk let an operator retain part of productivity gains. Price negotiation, excess capacity, Chinese competition, distributors, and specification-equivalent commodity pigments drive sharing at renewal.

caveats: No representative LMM synthetic-colorant AI adoption study was found.
The range excludes autonomous release decisions and hazardous process control.
rho.change_evidence: Measured deployments at batch specialty-chemical plants showing formulation cycle time, first-pass color match, laboratory hours, quality escapes, regulatory errors, scheduling performance, and staffing effects would materially move implementation.
rho.rationale: Search, documentation, specification review, formulation assistance, color prediction, scheduling, and commercial copilots are implementable within five years. Batch traceability, customer qualification, pigment-performance testing, wastewater compliance, and safe process control require validated data and human review; NIST identifies integration, reliability, explainability, and industrial-data barriers.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners across industries; 22% planned to sell or transfer. I use a similar base because plants, formulas, staff, approvals, and customer relationships can transfer and strategic chemical buyers exist, while environmental liabilities, family gifts, failed sales, and internal restructurings reduce qualifying outcomes. Net error direction is uncertain.
caveats: The source is cross-industry and includes firms below the LMM lens.
Stated owner plans are not completed qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, specialty-chemical broker, and strategic-acquirer records for completed and failed U.S. colorant transactions, paired with owner-age and succession data, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Qualified product portfolios and recurring industrial customers support transferability, but specialized permits, wastewater exposure, raw-material sourcing, plant condition, and customer requalification complicate ownership changes.

principal_driver: Qualification-bound repeat colorant demand and technical-service workflows create durable customer relationships and addressable indirect labor.
principal_weakness: The merged code spans very different pigment and dye niches, while contract economics and eligible-firm composition are not directly observed.

sources.supports: Broader chemical-manufacturing occupational mix used to bound wage-weighted task exposure.

sources.supports: Industrial AI opportunities and deployment barriers involving data, integration, reliability, explainability, and safety.
sources.title: 2026 Roadmap on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Smart Manufacturing

sources.supports: Pigment applications, customers, technical service, research roles, negotiated pricing, differentiation, competition, demand history, cyclicality, capacity, and current outlook.
sources.title: Kronos Worldwide 2025 Annual Report

sources.supports: Wastewater rules covering dyes and organic pigments and the complexity of batch specialty-chemical facilities.

sources.supports: U.S. employer-business five-year sale and transfer intentions.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Near-term U.S. coatings, specialty-chemical, basic-chemical, and downstream production-volume outlook.

sources.title: HHS, FDA to Phase Out Petroleum-Based Synthetic Dyes in Nation's Food Supply

title: Synthetic Dye and Pigment Manufacturing
